findings.description: BFS in reachable() enqueues every neighbor without ever consulting the `seen` set, so on cyclic input (explicitly permitted by its docstring) the loop never terminates and the queue grows until OOM, and even on DAGs each node is re-expanded once per distinct path (exponential time); fix is `if neighbor not in seen: seen.add(neighbor); queue.append(neighbor)` to restore O(V+E).

count_paths() recurses with no memoization, making runtime O(number of paths) — exponential on any DAG with diamond fan-in (~10^12 calls for 40 stacked diamonds) — and any accidental cycle or chain deeper than ~1000 nodes hits RecursionError; replace with memoized DP or counting along topological order for O(V+E).

_edges is a defaultdict(list) indexed directly on every read path (lines 30, 40, 54, 61), so read-only queries permanently insert an empty list (~120+ bytes) for each sink or unknown node probed — unbounded memory growth from queries and unsafe mutation under concurrent readers; use `self._edges.get(node, ())` on reads.
add_node() emits an INFO log per registered node, so at data-pipeline scale (10^5-10^6 nodes) graph construction is dominated by handler I/O and floods logs; drop to DEBUG or log aggregate counts.

add_edge() never deduplicates, so repeated registration of the same dependency in a long-running process grows adjacency lists without bound, multiplies traversal cost, and amplifies the exponential blowups in reachable()/count_paths(); low confidence since multigraph semantics may be intentional.

topological_order() seeds its queue from hash-randomized set iteration order, making output nondeterministic across processes (reproducibility risk for a pipeline) — noted briefly, detail deferred to critic-logic.
